metadata.summary: Designing XR (AR/VR/MR) interfaces that adapt to physical environment using AI scene understanding, gesture, voice, and graceful degradation.

content.title: AI + Spatial Computing
content.summary: Spatial interfaces generate complex contextual data that static UI rules cannot handle. AI scene classification enables UIs to auto-adapt layouts, pre-position relevant content, and anticipate user intent — reducing interaction friction in environments where traditional 2D patterns fail. Without explicit AI fallback design, failures in scene understanding or gesture prediction degrade UX to zero.

why.p: Spatial interfaces generate complex contextual data that static UI rules cannot handle. AI scene classification enables UIs to auto-adapt layouts, pre-position relevant content, and anticipate user intent — reducing interaction friction in environments where traditional 2D patterns fail. Without explicit AI fallback design, failures in scene understanding or gesture prediction degrade UX to zero.

list.item: Designing XR (AR/VR/MR) interfaces that must adapt to physical environment context
Building AI-driven spatial UI that pre-positions content based on scene understanding
Generating spatial layout specifications for Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest, or WebXR targets
Auditing existing spatial UI for contextual awareness gaps
Prototyping voice + gesture interaction flows for 3D environments
Standard 2D web/mobile UI — spatial computing overhead adds no value
Early concept validation — spatial UX requires hardware to meaningfully test
Budget-constrained projects where XR hardware deployment is not planned
Contexts where latency is unacceptable — AI scene understanding adds 50–200ms

section.name: gaze-target-sizing
section.p: Concrete rules for gaze-based interaction in spatial UX.

rule.statement: Define minimum gaze target size as 2 degrees of visual angle (approximately 35px at 1m on a typical HMD).
rule.rationale: Targets smaller than 2 degrees cause selection errors and eye strain. This is the platform-consistent minimum across visionOS, OpenXR, and Meta Quest guidelines.

section.name: depth-layers
section.p: Assigning UI elements to correct depth zones for legibility and comfort.

rule.statement: Assign UI to one of three depth zones: near (0.5m), mid (1–2m), far (4m+). Never mix reading distances within a single panel.
rule.rationale: Vergence-accommodation conflict causes discomfort when text at different focal distances appears in the same panel. Each zone has distinct legibility requirements and interaction models.

example.title: Depth Zone Assignments by Content Type
example.code: Near (0.5m) → Action panels, input fields, notifications Mid (1–2m) → Main content panels, data displays Far (4m+) → Ambient information, environmental context

section.name: world-anchoring
section.p: Anchoring UI to the world instead of the user's head.

rule.statement: Anchor persistent UI to the world (not the user's head) by default.
rule.rationale: Head-locked UI causes nausea in most users within minutes. World-anchored UI remains stable while the user moves, which is the expected behavior in spatial environments.

antipattern.description: Proposing head-locked UI panels for persistent content display.
antipattern.reason: Head-locked UI follows every head movement and induces nausea. Agents tend to propose head-locked layouts because they are simpler to specify — always flag and reject.

section.name: graceful-degradation
section.p: Designing fallback interactions when AI-driven features fail.

rule.statement: Design an explicit fallback for every AI-driven spatial adaptation. AI will fail; the fallback must produce acceptable UX without AI.
rule.rationale: AI scene understanding misclassifies rooms under poor lighting or cluttered environments. Voice integration fails in noisy public spaces. Gesture prediction degrades with non-standard postures. A spatial UI without fallbacks becomes unusable under these conditions.

rule.statement: Separate spatial layout from AI logic: layout specs must work without AI; AI adds enhancement only.
rule.rationale: AI-dependent layout creates a single point of failure. Base layout must be functional; AI state is an enhancement layer.

rule.statement: Include explicit confidence thresholds in all scene classification rules. AI outputs are probabilistic, not deterministic.
rule.rationale: Agents tend to produce hard scene-type mappings without confidence gates; low-confidence classifications applied without thresholds produce erratic UI behavior.

section.name: ai-capabilities
section.p: What AI can and cannot do in spatial computing: scene understanding, gesture prediction, voice, personalization.

list.item: Scene understanding — auto-adapts UI layout to detected room type (living room, office, kitchen, outdoor, unknown)
Object recognition — provides contextual information about physical objects in view
Gesture prediction — anticipates user intent from hand position and trajectory
Voice integration — accepts natural language commands as primary or fallback input
Personalization — learns user preferences over usage history

antipattern.description: Deploying AI scene understanding in environments with poor lighting or clutter without a default fallback layout.
antipattern.reason: Scene classification accuracy degrades significantly under non-ideal conditions; without a fallback the UI becomes unpredictable.

antipattern.description: Relying on gesture prediction as the primary input for users with non-standard postures or physical disabilities.
antipattern.reason: Gesture models are trained on normative postures; significant degradation with disability-related movement patterns makes gesture-only interaction exclusionary.

antipattern.description: Deploying voice integration as the only input modality in public or noisy environments.
antipattern.reason: Voice recognition fails in noisy spaces; a fallback interaction model (gaze + dwell, hand gesture) is mandatory.

antipattern.description: Personalization models without sufficient usage history.
antipattern.reason: Personalization models require sufficient usage history; cold-start UX is always degraded. Plan for graceful degradation in early-adoption phases.

antipattern.description: Ignoring battery and thermal constraints on standalone HMDs.
antipattern.reason: Battery and thermal constraints on standalone HMDs limit AI inference frequency. Design with inference budgets in mind.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: How agents can assist in spatial UI specification and validation.

A Claude subagent can generate spatial UI specifications (layout grids, depth layers, gaze target sizes) from a natural language brief and a target platform. For context-adaptive behaviors, the agent produces a decision matrix mapping detected scene types to UI states. Human review is mandatory before hardware prototyping because agents cannot run XR simulators or validate spatial ergonomics.

list.item: haiku — generating boilerplate spatial layout specs, depth-layer tables, gaze-target checklists
sonnet — scene-type decision matrices, AI capability mapping, interaction pattern documentation

antipattern.description: Agents cannot simulate XR hardware — all spatial ergonomic decisions require human sign-off before build.
antipattern.reason: Agents lack access to XR simulators; spatial design validation requires actual hardware or high-fidelity prototyping tools.

antipattern.description: Scene classification outputs from AI are probabilistic; agent specs must include explicit confidence thresholds and fallback rules.
antipattern.reason: Agents may omit confidence thresholds, leading to non-deterministic behavior.

antipattern.description: LLM hallucination risk is high for platform-specific constraints (e.g., visionOS eye-tracking limits) — always cite official platform docs.
antipattern.reason: Training data lag means agents may cite outdated API versions or incorrect platform limits.

antipattern.description: Personalization model designs produced by agents need human review for privacy compliance (GDPR/CCPA) before implementation.
antipattern.reason: Agents may not account for privacy regulations when proposing user data collection and retention patterns.

antipattern.description: Agents tend to propose head-locked UI (easy to specify) — flag and reject; world-anchored is always preferred.
antipattern.reason: Head-locked UI is simpler to define algorithmically; agents default to it without understanding nausea risk.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Tooling for spatial UI prototyping and debugging.

list.item: reality-composer-pro — Apple visionOS UI prototyping (Xcode toolchain, macOS only)
adb + Meta Developer Hub — Quest app sideloading and debugging (https://developer.meta.com/docs/)
spatialdocs (unofficial) — OpenXR spec CLI lookup (pip install spatialdocs)
webxr-emulator — Browser-based XR simulation (https://github.com/MozillaReality/WebXR-emulator-extension)

section.p: Services: Figma + Spatial Plugin (SaaS, partial agent compatibility, 2D mockups only), Unity with MRTK3 (OSS, partial, C# scripting), Needle Engine (SaaS/OSS, WebXR, REST API available), Gravity Sketch (SaaS, no API), ShapesXR (SaaS, collaborative, no agent API), Niantic Lightship VPS (SaaS, REST + SDK for scene anchoring).

list.item: Define gaze target minimum size as 2° of visual angle (≈ 35px at 1m on typical HMD)
Always design a graceful degradation path for every AI-driven adaptation — AI will fail
Use depth layers deliberately: UI at 0.5m (near), 1–2m (mid), 4m+ (far); never mix reading distances in one panel
Test voice commands with at least 3 non-native English speakers early — recognition degrades heavily with accents
Separate spatial layout from AI logic: layout specs should work without AI; AI adds enhancement only
Anchor persistent UI to world (not head) by default — head-locked UI causes nausea

example.title: Spatial UI Specification
example.p: You are designing a spatial UI for [platform: visionOS / Meta Quest / WebXR]. Environment: [room type or use case]. Generate: (1) depth layer assignments for each UI panel, (2) gaze target sizes in degrees of visual angle, (3) fallback interaction for voice failure, (4) AI adaptation rule for each scene type. Output as structured JSON.

example.title: Spatial UI Audit
example.p: Audit this spatial UI spec for: (1) missing fallback interactions, (2) gaze targets below 2° visual angle, (3) AI assumptions with no graceful degradation. List issues with severity High/Medium/Low.

template.purpose: Scene-type to UI state mapping generator
Prompt for generating spatial UI specifications
